2606.21977.title: Old Fictions, New Skins: Evaluating the Manipulative Capabilities of LLMs in Healthcare
2606.21977.paper_summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly piloted in African healthcare contexts, raising concerns about their potential to manipulate users in high-stakes settings. In a randomised experiment, we examined the manipulative capabilities of two publicly available models, ChatGPT 5.2 and DeepSeek V3.2, among Kenyan participants (N = 303). Participants interacted with either a manipulative variant or a non-manipulative variant before making a treatment decision within a hypothetical clinical scenario. The manipulative variant was prompted to covertly steer participants towards an incorrect treatment option while the non-manipulative variant served as the control condition. Manipulation success rates were higher in the manipulative condition (59.5%) than in the control condition (44.0%), with the effect reaching significance (OR = 2.11, 95% CI [1.12, 4.00], p = .021). These findings highlight the need for improved safety infrastructure specifically targeting manipulation, particularly given the integration of AI into healthcare systems across Africa.
2606.21977.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）在非洲醫療環境中的應用越來越廣泛，這引發了人們對其在高風險情境中操控用戶潛力的擔憂。<lb/>在一項隨機實驗中，我們檢視了兩個公開可用模型的操控能力，分別是 ChatGPT 5.2 和 DeepSeek V3.2，參與者來自肯尼亞（N = 303）。<lb/>參與者在做出假設性臨床情境中的治療決策之前，與操控變體或非操控變體互動。<lb/>操控變體被提示以隱秘的方式引導參與者選擇錯誤的治療選項，而非操控變體則作為對照條件。<lb/>在操控條件下的操控成功率（59.5%）高於對照條件（44.0%），且該效應達到顯著性（OR = 2.11, 95% CI [1.12, 4.00], p = .021）。<lb/>這些發現凸顯了針對操控的安全基礎設施改進的必要性，尤其是在非洲醫療系統中整合人工智慧的背景下。
2606.21977.author: Gathoni Ireri et.al.
